description: Bring a part into a wider arrangement, or coordinate several parts so they work within one whole. Integration concerns their functioning relation and can preserve their differences. Sameness and consent are separate relations.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade behind the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 'sh' and channel the air into high-front 'i'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', continuing into stressed middle-height back 'o'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l' and finish with rounded high-back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: Channelled friction, a rounded glide, and a lateral release ask three different gestures to cooperate. Their smooth succession recalls functional combination without making sameness its goal.
usage_notes: 'shiwolu' is transitive. 'koluma' (unite) joins people or things into one group, 'lorea' (connect) establishes a link, and 'pharune' (include) places something within a set. Assimilation and coercion need their own words.

examples.phi: ha terura lo phirae keli shiwolu.
examples.translation: This system integrates the different devices.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Integration can be discussed without hiding difference or consent. The word names how parts work within an arrangement; participation and coercion keep their own vocabulary.

semantic_domains.activity: coordinating parts within a wider arrangement
semantic_domains.cognition: bringing information or ideas into a coherent account
semantic_domains.community: participation of distinct people or groups within a shared structure
semantic_domains.creation: parts made to function within a constructed whole
